titleStmt.title: A Heracleopolite Sale of Barley on Delivery

encodingDesc.ab: Converted from a Word document for Pylon.

titlePart.seg: A Heracleopolite Sale of Barley on Delivery

div.p: The document was first published as a ‘Quittung über ein Getreidedarlehen’ (Gerstinger 1961: 59–60), later republished as SB 8 9773 under the title ‘Quittungshomologie über den Empfang eines Getreidedarlehens’. Its correct identification as a ‘Kauf bei Vorauszahlung’ was first recognized by P. J. Sijpesteijn, who also offered a number of corrections to the text (Sijpesteijn 1977: 98 n. 20, 108–109). Shortly thereafter, R. S. Bagnall and K. A. Worp made further improvements, establishing the date of the text as 540 (Bagnall and Worp 1978: 227–229), previously placed in 405. These corrections were not incorporated into the Berichtigungsliste, since a new edition in Sammelbuch had been envisaged (BL 7.214). No such edition has appeared, however, and the text as it currently stands cannot be used without consulting these two articles. While revising the text for DDbDP, it became clear that not all earlier proposals could be confirmed, that further emendations were required, and that, following autopsy, more than two dozen adjustments to brackets and underdots had to be made. Although the number of genuinely new readings is limited, the need for a reliable and readily accessible text justifies the present edition.

The document records that a priest of a church of Heracleopolis acknowledges receipt from Aurelia Manna of the full price of ten artabas of barley, paid in advance of delivery. The name of Manna’s father was intended to be added later but was never supplied. Six years earlier, in 534, Aurelia Manna, daughter of Ioannes and Ania, and Aurelia Nymphe, daughter of Apollos and Thamoule, appear as lessors of four aruras of land to the bishop of Heracleopolis, with a deacon of the cathedral church acting as guarantor for the women (SB 8 9876). It is likely that both texts concern the same Manna; taken together, they form a small dossier documenting the financial dealings of a propertied woman who repeatedly engaged with representatives of the local church. The circumstances underlying these transactions cannot be determined on present evidence.

note.p: 1–6 The reading of these lines is essentially after Bagnall and Worp 1978: 228. We have only transposed some dots and brackets.
5 Φαωφ[ι ̣ ̣ ̣ ̣]ῃ̣: Φαωφ[ι εἰκάδ]ι Bagnall and Worp 1978: 228. [πρώτ]ῃ̣, [τρίτ]ῃ̣, [ἕκτ]ῃ̣, [ὀγδό]ῃ̣ (28, 30 September; 3, 5 October) would fit.
10–11 Cf. SB 8 9876.5–6 (534) Αὐρη]λίαι Μάννα θυγάτηρ Ἰωάννου, μητρὸς | Ἀνίας.
11 The vacat was first recognized by Sijpesteijn 1977: 109.
15 ⟨ἀρταβῶν⟩ δέκα μέ̣τ̣ρῳ̣ is due to Sijpesteijn 1977: 109.

18 καρπῶν. The first edition restored γεννημάτων, which is unparalleled and too long for the available space. The phrase ἀπὸ τῶν καρπῶν is not attested in this position in any of the few Heracleopolite documents of this type (SPP 20 148.15 has only καρπῶν), though it is known from other regions, including the neighbouring Fayum; cf. e.g. CPR 10 23.7 (Ars.; 523).

19–20 [ἀνυπερθέτως καὶ] ἄ̣ν̣ευ πά[ση]ς̣ | [ἀντιλογίας. τὸ γραμ]μ̣άτιον [κύριον]. Sijpesteijn 1977: 108 proposed [ἀνυπερθέτως καὶ ἄν]ευ πά[σης ἀντιλογίας] | [τῆς πράξεώς σοι οὔσης] κ̣α̣ὶ̣ τ̣ο̣[ῖς παρὰ σοῦ] (he numbered these lines as 18–19) There is no room for ἀντιλογίας in l. 19, and it is unlikely that the word was divided between the lines. Furthermore, there is a tiny horizontal stroke immediately after the lacuna, which we take to belong to the extended cap of the final sigma of πά[ση]ς̣. In l. 20, κ̣α̣ὶ̣ τ̣ο̣[ῖς is not a possible reading; ed. pr. read το]ῦ̣ ἁγίου, which is close to what the papyrus has but not correct. We read γραμ]μ̣άτιον, as expected at this point in Heracleopolite documents; cf. e.g. SPP 20 148.19 (6th c.). This formulation is another indication that P.Hoogendijk 39, endorsed Ἡ]ρ̣ακλ( ), is Heracleopolite.

21 [καὶ ἐπερ(ωτηθεὶς) ὡμολ(όγησα). Ἰο]ύ̣λ̣ι̣ο̣[ς]. The first edition read [δι’ ἐμοῦ ]ου ἐ̣[τελειώθη], while Sijpesteijn 1977: 108 stated that the traces were ‘zu undeutlich, um eine Deutung zu wagen’.

A composite of two minute, possibly contiguous fragments mounted in the same frame as fr. 1. ]κει in l. 1 could be part of προκείμενος, which would have belonged to Ioulios’ declaration and l. 21, that is, [πρεσβύτερος υἱὸς Ἀπανακίου ὁ προ]κεί|[μενος], but the remains of the next two lines cannot be placed in this context. An alternative is ὡς πρό]κει|[ται, from the end of the declaration, but it is again impossible to reconstruct a text that could include the rest of the fragment.

χι(ρόγραφον): ὁμο]λ(ογία) ed. pr. The monogram chi-iota for χειρόγραφον is standard in endorsements of Arsinoite and Heracleopolite documents in this period; see Monte (2024), with a list of attestations in Table 1.1, pp. 197–200.
ἀπ̣[ὸ] κώμ̣η̣[ς Πεενη] Sijpesteijn 1977: 98 n. 20, who further notes that the name of the village may have been followed by a reference to the nome.

listBibl.bibl: Bagnall, R. S. and K. A. Worp (1978) “Ten Consular Dates,” ZPE 28: 221–230.
Gerstinger, H. (1961) “Neue byzantinische Vertragsurkunden aus der Sammlung „Papyri Erzherzog Rainer“ in Wien,” JJP 13: 53–65.

Monte, A. (2024) “Le abbreviazioni monogrammatiche chi-iota e chi-rho per χειρόγραφον nei papiri greci di epoca bizantina (IV-VII sec. d.C.),” in M. Boccuzzi (ed.), Mani e scritture, simboli e testi. Ricerche su fonti documentarie ed epigrafiche tardoantiche, Roma: 195–211.
Sijpesteijn, P. J. (1977) “Addenda et Corrigenda zu Wiener Texten,” ZPE 24: 95–109.

encodingDesc.p: This file encoded to comply with EpiDoc Guidelines and Schema version 8

availability.p: © Duke Databank of Documentary Papyri. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 License.

div.ab:
βασιλείας τοῦ θειοτάτου καὶ εὐσεβεστάτου
ἡμῶν δεσπότου Φλαουΐου Ἰουστινιανοῦ
τοῦ αἰωνίου Αὐγούστου καὶ Αὐτοκράτορος
ἔτους ιδ ὑπατείας Φλαουΐου Ἰουστίνου
τοῦ ἐνδοξοτάτου Φαωφι ῃ τῆς
εὐτυχοῦς τετάρτης ἰνδικτίονος ἐν Ἡρακλέους
πόλει. Ἰούλιος πρεσβύτερος τῆς ἁγίας
ἐκκλησίας, υἱὸς Ἀπανακίου μητρὸς
Μαρίας ἀπὸ κώμης Πεενη τοῦ Ἡρακλεο
πολίτου νομοῦ Αὐρηλίᾳ Μάννᾳ
θυγατρὶ ἀφ’ Ἡρακλέους πόλεως
χαίρειν. ὁμολογῶ ἐσχηκέναι παρὰ σοῦ
διὰ χειρὸς ἐξ οἴκου καὶ πεπληρῶσθαι
τὴν τιμὴν κριθῶν ἐπιτηδείων
ἀρταβῶν δέκα μέτρῳ τῷ ἐμῷ καὶ τὴν τούτων
ἀπόδοσίν σοι ποιήσομαι μετὰ πίστεως
ἀγαθῆς τῷ Παυνι μηνὶ ἀπὸ τῶν
καρπῶν τῆς πέμπτης ἰνδικτίονος
ἀνυπερθέτως καὶ ἄνευ πάσης
ἀντιλογίας. τὸ γραμμάτιον κύριον
καὶ ἐπερωτηθεὶς ὡμολόγησα. Ἰούλιος
χειρόγραφονχιρόγραφον Ἰουλίου υἱοῦ Ἀπανακίου πρεσβυτέρου ἀπὸ κώμης Πεενη

availability.p: © Heidelberger Gesamtverzeichnis der griechischen Papyrusurkunden Ägyptens. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 License.

sourceDesc.p: The contents of this document are generated from SOSOL.

div.p: In the reign of our most godly and most pious master, Flavius Iustinianus, the eternal Augustus and Imperator, year 14, in the consulship of Flavius Iustinus, vir gloriosissimus, Phaophi …, of the auspicious fourth indiction, at Heracleopolis. I, Ioulios, priest of the Holy Church, son of Apanakios, mother Maria, from the village of Peene of the Heracleopolite nome, to Aurelia Manna daughter of … from Heracleopolis, greetings. I acknowledge that I have received from you, by hand, from (your) house, and have been paid in full the price of ten arabas of quality barley, (measured) by my own measure, and I will deliver them to you in good faith in the month of Pauni from the produce of the fifth indiction, with no delay and without any dispute. The deed is binding, and in reply to the formal question I assented. I, Ioulios, … Back: Chirograph of Ioulios son of Apanakios, priest, from the village of Peene …
